validation.allScoreDefinitionNamesShouldBeUnique: All score definition names should be unique
validation.traceVariableKeyInvalid: Key is invalid, it should be "input", "output", "metadata" (e.g. "input.message" or just "input" for the whole object), or the reserved word "spans" to inject the trace's spans list
validation.spanVariableKeyInvalid: Key is invalid, it should be "input", "output", "metadata", and follow this format: "input.[PATH]" For example: "input.message" or just "input" for the whole object

annotationQueues.annotationGuidelinesDescription: Set how items are scored and labeled, and provide instructions so annotators give consistent feedback.
annotationQueues.shareAnnotationQueueDescription: You must invite annotators to your workspace for them to review the items. After creating the queue, you'll get a direct link to share with them.
annotationQueues.annotationQueueLinkCopied: Annotation queue link copied to clipboard
annotationQueues.annotationQueueLinkCopiedDescription: Share this queue with your annotators so they can start annotating and provide feedback to improve the evaluation of your LLM application.
annotationQueues.deleteAnnotationQueueDescription: Deleting an annotation queue will permanently remove it from the system. All associated data and configurations will be lost. This action can't be undone. Are you sure you want to continue?
annotationQueues.deleteAnnotationQueuesDescription: Deleting these annotation queues will permanently remove all associated annotations and reviews. This action cannot be undone. Are you sure you want to continue?
annotationQueues.noInstructionsProvided: No instructions were provided for this annotation queue
annotationQueues.organizeYourAnnotationsDescription: Annotation queues let you organize traces for review and labeling, making it easy to collect feedback and improve LLM outputs.
annotationQueues.commentsDescription: Text field for open feedback or additional notes.

automations.enableRuleDescription: Enable or disable this evaluation rule
automations.codeMetricCannotBeUpdated: Code metric cannot be updated. This feature is not available for this environment
automations.scopeTooltip: Choose whether the evaluation rule scores the entire thread, each individual trace, or each span. Thread-level rules assess the full conversation, trace-level rules evaluate one model response at a time, and span-level rules evaluate individual operations within a trace.
automations.youReAboutToLoseChanges: You're about to lose your changes
automations.loseChangesDescription:
If you change the evaluation scope, your current rule settings — including prompt, model, and variable mappings — will be reset.
Are you sure you want to continue?
automations.filteringAndSamplingTraceDescription: Apply filters and sampling to select which traces will be evaluated by this rule
automations.filteringAndSamplingThreadDescription: Use sampling rate to control how frequently this rule is applied to threads
automations.filteringAndSamplingSpanDescription: Apply filters and sampling to select which spans will be evaluated by this rule
automations.samplingRateDescription: Use sampling rate to control how frequently this rule is applied. You can also add filters to select specific {{scope}} based on their properties. If nothing is defined, the rule will evaluate all {{scope}}.
automations.pythonCodeVariablesDescription: All variables are automatically added based on the code snippet. They are extracted from the `score` method and are required.
automations.pythonCodeParsingError: Code parsing error. The variables cannot be extracted.
automations.noOnlineEvaluationRules: No online evaluation rules assigned to this project
automations.runOnlineEvaluationDescription: Choose the online evaluation rules you want to apply to the selected {{entity}}. Each rule will generate new scores based on its configuration.
automations.createNewRuleDescription: Create a new rule, or assign an existing rule in the Online evaluation page
automations.invalidFilterKeyError: Key is invalid, it should be "input", "output", and follow this format: "input.[PATH]" For example: "input.message" or just "input" for the whole object

trigger.traceErrorsDescription: Triggered when the number of trace errors exceeds the specified threshold in selected projects.
trigger.guardrailTriggeredDescription: Triggered when a guardrail event occurs in any trace within the selected projects.
trigger.traceFeedbackScoreDescription: Triggered when the average feedback score for traces exceeds the specified threshold in selected projects.
trigger.threadFeedbackScoreDescription: Triggered when the average feedback score for threads exceeds the specified threshold in selected projects.
trigger.promptCreatedDescription: Triggered when a new prompt is created or saved in the workspace's prompt library.
trigger.promptCommittedDescription: Triggered when a new commit (version) is added to any prompt in the workspace's prompt library.
trigger.promptDeletedDescription: Triggered when a prompt is removed from the workspace's prompt library.
trigger.experimentFinishedDescription: Triggered when an experiment completes in the workspace.
trigger.costThresholdDescription: Triggered when total trace cost exceeds the specified threshold in the selected projects.
trigger.latencyThresholdDescription: Triggered when average trace latency exceeds the specified threshold in the selected projects.

evaluateTraces.noTracesAssociated: No traces are associated with this experiment.
evaluateTraces.failedToLoadTraces: Failed to load experiment traces for evaluation.

smeFlow.queueErrorAlert: Unable to load annotation queue. The queue may not exist or you may not have access to it.
smeFlow.queueNotAvailableDescription: Please check the queue link or contact your administrator for access.
smeFlow.itemsErrorAlert: Unable to load items in this annotation queue. This is usually a temporary issue.
smeFlow.itemsErrorDescription: Please try again. If the problem persists, contact your administrator.
smeFlow.noAnnotationQueueSelectedDescription: To begin SME evaluation, please access this page through an annotation queue link.

hotkeys.goToNextItemToReview: Go to next item to review

playground.noRunsYetDescription: Run a prompt to see results.

explainers.visibleScores: Only the scores you select here will be visible to annotators during evaluation
explainers.whatDoYouUseProjectsFor: Set up a new project to organize, isolate, and monitor different LLM workflows and apps.
explainers.whatAreFeedbackScores: Feedback scores are evaluations of your LLM outputs, obtained via human review, online evaluation rules, or the SDK
explainers.iCreatedAProjectNowWhat: Log traces to your project to capture LLM interactions, analyze performance, run experiments, and improve your application over time.
explainers.whatAreTraces: A trace is a step-by-step record of how your LLM application processes a single input, including LLM calls and other operations.
explainers.whatAreSpans: A span represents a single step in the execution of a trace. Use spans to debug, monitor, and evaluate model behavior.
explainers.whatAreThreads: A thread represents a full conversation session, grouping together multiple related traces. Use threads to review and evaluate entire interactions in chat-based applications.
explainers.whatsOnlineEvaluation: Automatically score your production traces by defining LLM-as-a-Judge or code metrics.
explainers.iAddedTracesToATestSuiteNowWhat: Your traces have been added to the test suite and can now be used in experiments to evaluate your agent.
explainers.iAddedItemsToADatasetNowWhat: Your items have been added to the dataset and can now be used in experiments to evaluate your agent.
explainers.whyWouldIWantToAddTracesToATestSuite: Add traces to a test suite to evaluate and benchmark LLM outputs using real production data. You can then use these test suites in experiments to track how your LLM app's performance evolves over time.
explainers.howsTheCostEstimated: Opik estimates the cost of each trace by calculating token usage across all LLM calls, using model-specific pricing.
explainers.howsTheThreadCostEstimated: Opik estimates the cost of each thread by calculating token usage across all Traces and associated LLM calls, applying model-specific pricing.
explainers.whatsThatPromptSelect: Select the LLM-as-a-Judge prompt to use. You can use one of the prompts provided by Opik, or you can [create your own metrics].
explainers.iAddedEditedANewOnlineEvaluationRuleNowW: All new traces will now be automatically scored using this rule. You can view the results in the Traces table, the Metrics tab, and in each trace's detail view.
explainers.iAddedEditedANewOnlineEvaluationThreadLe: All new threads will now be automatically scored using this rule. You can view the results in the Threads table, the Metrics tab, and in each thread's detail view.
explainers.iAddedEditedANewOnlineEvaluationSpanLeve: All new spans will now be automatically scored using this rule. You can view the results in the Spans table, the Metrics tab, and in each span's detail view.
explainers.whatAreTheseElementsInTheTree: The span tree visualizes the sequence and structure of operations in a trace - including LLM calls, tool calls, guardrails, and more.
explainers.whatIsHumanReview: Human review fields let you manually rate LLM outputs. Use them to gather structured feedback and monitor quality over time.
explainers.whatAreAnnotationQueues: Add traces or thread to an annotation queue to collect human feedback on your LLM outputs. Only queues created in this project appear here, and traces can be added to them only
explainers.howToChooseAnnotationQueueType: Choose whether the annotation queue will contain the entire thread or individual traces
explainers.whatsAnExperiment: Experiments help you test prompts and models, compare versions, and track improvements or issues.
explainers.whatsAPromptCommit: Prompt commits capture the exact prompt version used in an experiment, ensuring reproducibility and traceability of results. Manage your prompts in the Prompt library.
explainers.whatAreExperimentItems: Experiment items are individual evaluations that connect a dataset sample with its LLM output, feedback scores, and trace.
explainers.whatsTheExperimentConfiguration: The experiment configuration captures key settings, like the prompt, model, and temperature, to keep experiments reproducible and easy to analyze.
explainers.whatDoesItMeanToCompareMyExperiments: Compare experiments to understand how changes to prompts, models, or rules impact performance. Select at least two experiments from the same test suite to get started.
explainers.whatsTheTestSuiteItem: Test suite items are individual input samples in an experiment, each representing a test case your LLM app processes and evaluates.
explainers.whatsATestSuite: A test suite is a collection of input-output examples used to evaluate your LLM application's performance.
explainers.whyDoINeedMultipleTestSuites: Create datasets and test suites to evaluate your LLM application in different contexts, scenarios, or environments.
explainers.whatFormatIsThisToAddMyTestSuiteItem: Define the input data for triggering your agent. Use a valid JSON object with key-value pairs. Example: {"key": "value"}
explainers.whatFormatIsThisToAddMyDatasetItem: Define input-output examples for evaluating your agent. Use a valid JSON object with key-value pairs. Example: {"key": "value"}
explainers.whatsThePromptLibrary: Store and version prompts to maintain consistency and simplify experimentation across projects
explainers.howDoIUseThisPrompt: To use a saved prompt from your library, select it when configuring an experiment or in the playground. You can also reference its latest version or a specific commit.
explainers.whyDoIHaveExperimentsInThePromptLibrary: Viewing experiments that used the same prompt helps identify how prompt variations impact results and refine your approach.
explainers.whatAreCommits: Each edit in a prompt creates a new commit, ensuring version control and reproducibility.
explainers.howDoIWriteMyPrompt: Write prompts as your LLM would receive them, including any dynamic placeholders or formatting.
explainers.whatHappensIfIEditMyPrompt: Write prompts as your LLM would receive them, including any dynamic placeholders or formatting. Editing creates a new version automatically. View history in the Commits tab.
explainers.whatsThePlayground: Test before saving a prompt to the library or running a full experiment. Or use it to explore different prompt variations and see how your LLM responds in real time.
explainers.whatsTheseConfigurationThings: The model configuration parameters allow you to control output behavior. Tuning these lets you balance between deterministic answers and more diverse, exploratory outputs.
explainers.whyDoINeedAnAiProvider: Connect AI providers to test prompts, preview model responses, and score traces using online evaluation rules in the Playground.
explainers.whatDoesTheTestSuiteDoHere: Run a prompt on your test suite to preview its performance. You can select metrics, filter the data, or adjust the page size. The experiment runs only on the items currently shown.
explainers.howDoIUseTheTestSuiteInThePlayground: Use mustache syntax to reference test suite variables in your prompt. Example:
explainers.whatsLlmAsAJudge: LLM-as-a-Judge uses a language model to score outputs based on custom natural language criteria like relevance, clarity, or factual accuracy.
explainers.whatsACodeMetric: A code metric is a Python function that scores LLM outputs using logic-based rules - like exact matches or keyword checks. Ideal for fast, deterministic evaluations without relying on another LLM.
explainers.whatAreFeedbackDefinitions: Create custom fields to manually rate LLM outputs. Use them to collect structured feedback and track quality over time.
explainers.whatFormatShouldTheMetadataBe: Use a valid JSON object with key-value pairs. Example: {"key": "value"}
explainers.whatFormatShouldThePromptBe: Use mustache syntax to reference test suite variables in your prompt. Example: {{question}}.
explainers.whatsAnOptimizationRun: Automatically test prompt variations and find the best-performing one.
explainers.whatsTheBestScore: Highest evaluation result achieved during the optimization run, based on your selected metric.
explainers.whatHappensIfIEditAnAiProvider: A key is already set for this provider. Since AI provider configurations are workspace-wide, adding a new key will overwrite the existing one for all users.
explainers.whatHappensIfIEditARule: Changes will only apply to new traces. Existing traces won't be affected.
explainers.whatHappensIfIEditAThreadRule: Changes will only apply to new threads. Existing threads won't be affected.
explainers.whatHappensIfIEditAFeedbackDefinition: Changes will only apply to new feedback scores. Existing feedback scores won't be affected.
explainers.whyWouldIWantToCreateANewProject: Organize your work by app, integration, or environment. Projects keep traces, metrics, and evaluations separate, making it easier to manage, monitor, and collaborate.
explainers.whatsTheCommitHistory: The commit history tracks every change made to a prompt, including edits, timestamps, and authors.
explainers.whyWouldICompareCommits: Compare prompt commits to understand how changes affect output quality and performance. Select at least two commits to get started.
explainers.whatsTheOptimizer: An optimizer is a built-in algorithm from the Opik Agent Optimizer SDK that improves prompt effectiveness. Each one uses its own strategy and configurable settings to target specific optimization goals.
explainers.whatAreTrialItems: Trial items are test suite samples processed during a trial. Each one generates an output and score that contribute to the trial's results.
explainers.whatsTheEvaluationRunConfiguration: The evaluation run configuration captures key settings - like the metric, and optimizer - to keep evaluation runs reproducible and easy to analyze.
explainers.metricEquals: Checks if the output exactly matches an expected string. Use this for strict equality checks.
explainers.metricContains: Checks if the output contains a specific substring, can be both case sensitive or case insensitive.
explainers.metricRegexMatch: Checks if the output matches a specified regular expression pattern.
explainers.metricIsJson: Checks if the output is a valid JSON object.
explainers.metricLevenshtein: Calculates the Levenshtein distance between the output and an expected string.
explainers.metricSentenceBleu: Calculates a single-sentence BLEU score for a candidate vs. one or more references.
explainers.metricCorpusBleu: Calculates a corpus-level BLEU score for multiple candidates vs. their references.
explainers.metricRouge: Calculates the ROUGE score for a candidate vs. one or more references.
explainers.metricHallucination: The hallucination metric allows you to check if the LLM response contains any hallucinated information. In order to check for hallucination, you will need to provide the LLM input, LLM output. If the context is provided, this will also be used to check for hallucinations.
explainers.metricGEval: G-Eval is a task agnostic LLM as a Judge metric that allows you to specify a set of criteria for your metric and it will use a Chain of Thought prompting technique to create some evaluation steps and return a score.
explainers.metricModeration: The Moderation metric allows you to evaluate the appropriateness of the LLM's response to the given LLM output. It does this by asking the LLM to rate the appropriateness of the response on a scale of 1 to 10, where 1 is the least appropriate and 10 is the most appropriate.
explainers.metricUsefulness: The usefulness metric allows you to evaluate how useful an LLM response is given an input. It uses a language model to assess the usefulness and provides a score between 0.0 and 1.0, where higher values indicate higher usefulness. Along with the score, it provides a detailed explanation of why that score was assigned.
explainers.metricAnswerRelevance: The Answer Relevance metric allows you to evaluate how relevant and appropriate the LLM's response is to the given input question or prompt. To assess the relevance of the answer, you will need to provide the LLM input (question or prompt) and the LLM output (generated answer). Unlike the Hallucination metric, the Answer Relevance metric focuses on the appropriateness and pertinence of the response rather than factual accuracy.
explainers.metricContextPrecision: The context precision metric evaluates the accuracy and relevance of an LLM's response based on provided context, helping to identify potential hallucinations or misalignments with the given information.
explainers.metricContextRecall: The context recall metric evaluates the accuracy and relevance of an LLM's response based on provided context, helping to identify potential hallucinations or misalignments with the given information.
explainers.traceOpikAi: Our AI assistant allows you to analyze trace and spans data (which may include personal or sensitive information) using a generative AI model via OpenAI, L.L.C.
explainers.feedbackScoresHotkeys:
Press "F" to jump to first component in feedback scores
and use "Tab"/"Shift+Tab" to move between components

Controller Types:

1. Numerical Inputs (number fields):
   - use arrow keys to adjust values
   - or type numbers directly

2. Dropdown selectors:
   - press "Enter" to open select
   - use arrow keys to navigate
   - press "Enter" to select

3. Toggle buttons:
   - use arrow keys to navigate between options
   - press "Enter" to select

4. Reason Text Areas (comment fields):
   - press "Enter" to open/close section
   - type your reasoning
explainers.llmJudgeVariableMapping: Choose the trace field that should fill each variable. Map variables to any trace field, including image fields like input.image_url or output.image_base64.
explainers.promptGenerationLearnMore: Not sure where to start? Tell us your goal in plain language, and we'll generate a ready-to-use prompt.
explainers.promptImprovementLearnMore: Give your prompt a boost! Add optional guidance, or let AI apply best-practice improvements for you.
explainers.promptImprovementOptimizer: Looking for advanced optimization algorithms? Check out the Opik optimizer!
explainers.whatsAnAlert: Monitor important events in your project and get notified when something needs your attention.
explainers.whatAreDashboards: Set up dashboards to monitor quality, cost, and performance of your projects and share experiment results
explainers.whatsTheOptimizationConfig: Configure your setup and let Opik automatically find the best prompt.
explainers.whatsTheAlgorithmSection: How Opik explores and improves prompt variations.
explainers.whatsTheTestSuiteSection: Data Opik uses to evaluate prompt variations.
explainers.whatsTheMetricSection: How Opik measures and compares prompt performance.
explainers.whatsTheMetricSettings: Configure parameters for the selected evaluation metric to customize how your outputs are scored.
explainers.whatsTheAlgorithmSettings: Configure parameters for the selected optimization algorithm to control how prompts are improved.
explainers.gevalTaskIntroduction: Provide context about the task being evaluated.
explainers.gevalEvaluationCriteria: Define specific criteria for evaluating the output quality.
explainers.metricReferenceKey: The key in the test suite item to compare against. Supports JSONPath for nested values (e.g., $.scores[?(@.name=='Useful')].value).
explainers.metricCaseSensitive: Enable case-sensitive comparison when evaluating outputs.
explainers.optimizerVerbose: Enable detailed logging during optimization process.
explainers.optimizerAdaptiveMutation: Automatically adjust mutation rate based on optimization progress.
explainers.optimizerEnableLlmCrossover: Use LLM to intelligently combine solutions.
explainers.optimizerOutputStyleGuidance: Optional guidance on desired output format or style.
explainers.optimizerInferOutputStyle: Automatically detect and maintain output style from examples.
explainers.feedbackScoreGroupbyRequiresSingleMetric: Group by is available only when exactly one metric is selected. Select a single metric from the Metrics dropdown to enable grouping.
explainers.durationGroupbyRequiresSingleMetric: Group by is available only when exactly one duration percentile is selected. Select a single percentile from the Duration metrics dropdown to enable grouping.
explainers.usageGroupbyRequiresSingleMetric: Group by is available only when exactly one usage metric is selected. Select a single metric from the Usage metrics dropdown to enable grouping.
explainers.groupbyRequiresMetric: Select a metric type first to enable grouping.

providers.opikFreeDescription: Free model provided by Opik - no API key required
providers.ollamaDescription: Run open-source LLMs locally with Ollama. Connect to your local or cloud Ollama instance.
providers.customDescription:
You can configure any OpenAI API-compatible provider (vLLM, 
Ollama, etc.) using the standardized OpenAI API interface.

templates.customLlmAsJudgeDescription: Use our template editor to write your own LLM as a Judge metric
templates.hallucinationDescription: Check if the output contains any hallucinations
templates.moderationDescription: Check if the output contains any harmful content
templates.answerRelevanceDescription: Check if the output is relevant to the input
templates.structuredOutputComplianceDescription: Checks if the output follows a defined JSON or JSON-LD structure
templates.meaningMatchDescription: Evaluates semantic equivalence between output and ground truth
templates.conversationalCoherenceDescription: Check if the output is coherent with the conversation
templates.userFrustrationDescription: Check if the output is frustrating to the user

schemaDescriptions.correctness: Whether the assistant's output effectively addresses the user's input
schemaDescriptions.correctnessSpan: Whether the span's output effectively addresses the span's input
schemaDescriptions.relevance: Whether the LLM response is relevant to the conversation context
schemaDescriptions.hallucination: Hallucination score identifies factual errors in the response
schemaDescriptions.moderation: Moderation score identifies harmful content in the response
schemaDescriptions.answerRelevance: Answer relevance score checks if the output is relevant to the question
schemaDescriptions.structureCompliance: Returns True if the output follows the expected structure
schemaDescriptions.meaningMatch: Evaluate if the output conveys the same meaning as the ground truth
schemaDescriptions.userFrustration: User frustration score checks if the output is frustrating to the user

gepa.label: GEPA optimizer
gepa.description: Tries many prompt variations and keeps the best ones.

hierarchicalReflective.label: Hierarchical Reflective
hierarchicalReflective.description: Analyzes failures and rewrites prompts to fix issues.

equals.label: Equals
equals.description: Checks for an exact match with the expected output.

jsonSchemaValidator.label: JSON Schema Validator
jsonSchemaValidator.description: Validates output structure against a JSON schema.

gEval.label: Custom (G-Eval)
gEval.description: Uses an LLM to score outputs with custom criteria.

levenshtein.label: Levenshtein
levenshtein.description: Measures edit distance between output and expected text.

numericalSimilarity.label: Numerical Similarity
numericalSimilarity.description: Compares numeric output against a reference value. Closer values score higher.

demoTemplate.title: Demo template - Opik Chatbot
demoTemplate.description: Train a chatbot to answer Opik questions and decline off-topic requests

prompts.unsavedTooltip: This prompt version hasn't been saved
prompts.unsavedExperimentTooltip: This prompt version hasn't been saved. Save it to link it to the experiment and make comparisons easier.

testSuites.passCriteriaDescription: Set the number of runs per item and the minimum required to pass.

openclaw.installDescription: Run this in your OpenClaw Gateway environment to install the Opik plugin.
openclaw.step2Description: The setup wizard validates your endpoint and credentials, then writes config under plugins.entries.opik-openclaw.
openclaw.step3Description: Print the active Opik configuration to verify the setup.
openclaw.step4Title: 4. Start the gateway and send a test message
openclaw.step4Description: Traces will stream into your "PROJECT_NAME_PLACEHOLDER" Opik project.

permissions.disabledDatasetsTooltip: You don't have permission to view datasets
permissions.disabledExperimentsTooltip: You don't have permission to view experiments

shared.installOpikSectionTitle: 1. Install Opik using pip from the command line
shared.installOpikDefaultDescription: Install Opik from the command line using pip.

explainers.whyWouldIWantToAddTracesToATestSuite: Add traces to a test suite to evaluate your agent's performance using real production data.

guardrails.topicGuardrailHint: The topic guardrail is designed to prevent the model from generating responses on certain topics that might be inappropriate, unsafe, unethical, or outside its intended scope.
guardrails.piiGuardrailHint: The PII (Personally Identifiable Information) guardrail is designed to prevent the model from generating, storing, or processing sensitive personal data that could identify individuals.

agentConfig.fieldNamePattern: Use letters, digits and underscore; start with a letter or underscore
agentConfig.fieldNameAlreadyExists: A field with this name already exists
agentConfig.duplicateFieldName: Duplicate field name in the new fields
